coredata.dc:title: Carbon-dot-based dual-emission silica nanoparticles as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for Bisphenol A
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Xiang, Guo-Qiang
Ren, Yue
Xia, Yin
Mao, Wenjie
Fan, Chao
Guo, Si-Yu
Wang, Pan-Pan
Yang, Deng-Hui
He, Lijun
Jiang, Xiuming
coredata.dc:description: A simple and effective strategy for designing a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor is described in this work. A carbon dots (CDs) based dual-emission nanosensor for Bisphenol A (BPA) was prepared by coating CDs on the surface of dye-doped silica nanoparticles. The fluorescence of dual-emission silica nanoparticles was quenched in hydrochloric acid by potassium bromate (KBrO3) oxidation; BPA inhibited KBrO3 oxidation, resulting in the ratiometric fluorescence response of dual-emission silica nanoparticles. Several important parameters affecting the performance of the nanosensor were investigated and optimized. The detection limit of this nanosensor was 0.80ng mL−1 with a linear range from 10 to 500ng mL−1. This was applied successfully to determine BPA in the leached solution of different plastic products with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Carbon dots
Bisphenol A
Silica nanoparticles

Characterization of CD-Coated Dual-Emission Silica Nanoparticles
Sensing Mechanism of the Ratiometric Nanosensor for BPA
Selection of Acid Media and Concentration of the System
Effect of the Concentration of KBrO3
Reaction Temperature and Time of the Inhibiting System

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the preparation of CD-coated dual emission silica nanoparticles.
FT-IR spectra of CDs (c), RhB-doped silica nanoparticles (a) and CD-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles (b); TEM image of CD-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles.

Fluorescence spectra of the analytical system. (CD stand for CD-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles solution (1.0mgmL−1), CD+HCl+KBrO3 stand for CD with KBrO3 (2.4×10−6 M) in 0.2M HCl solution, CD+HCl+KBrO3 +BPA stand for CD with KBrO3 (2.4×10−6 M) (in 0.2M HCl) and different concentrations of BPA solutions.

Effect of acid type on the ΔF of the system (CD-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles: 1.0mgmL−1; KBrO3: 6.0×10−6 M; a-HCl, b-H2SO4, and c-H3PO4: 0.2M; BPA: 300ngmL−1; Reaction temperature: 20°C; Reaction time: 20min).

Effect of the concentration of KBrO3 on the ΔF of the system. (CD-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles: 1.0mgmL−1; KBrO3: 3.0×10−7- 4.2×10−6 M; HCl, 0.2M; BPA: 300ngmL−1; Reaction temperature: 20°C; Reaction time: 20min).

Effect of reaction temperature on the ΔF of the system. (CD-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles:1.0mgmL−1; KBrO3: 2.0×10−6 M; HCl: 0.2M; BPA: 300ngmL−1; Reaction temperature: 20–90°C; Reaction time: 20min).
Analysis results for the determination of BPA in the leached solutions of plastic products (n=3, mean±SD, ng mL−1).

head.ce:title: Carbon-dot-based dual-emission silica nanoparticles as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for Bisphenol A

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University of technology, Zhengzhou, 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and effective strategy for designing a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor is described in this work. A carbon dots (CDs) based dual-emission nanosensor for Bisphenol A (BPA) was prepared by coating CDs on the surface of dye-doped silica nanoparticles. The fluorescence of dual-emission silica nanoparticles was quenched in hydrochloric acid by potassium bromate (KBrO3) oxidation; BPA inhibited KBrO3 oxidation, resulting in the ratiometric fluorescence response of dual-emission silica nanoparticles. Several important parameters affecting the performance of the nanosensor were investigated and optimized. The detection limit of this nanosensor was 0.80ng mL−1 with a linear range from 10 to 500ng mL−1. This was applied successfully to determine BPA in the leached solution of different plastic products with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol A (2,2-bis(4-hydroxyphenyl)propane, BPA) has been widely used as an intermediate in the manufacture of polycarbonate (PC), epoxy resins, and other plastics [1,2]. These polymers are used in diverse food contact materials such as tableware, storage containers, water pipes, infant feeding bottles, and protective linings for beverage cans [3]. Since the first report of the release of BPA from PC bottles at high temperatures and pressures, BPA toxicity has attracted much attention [4,5]. A large number of studies have shown that BPA can mimic and interfere with hormonal activities by disrupting growth, development, and reproduction [6]; most importantly, BPA was found to possibly cause cancer [7]. A review of the key evidence on the hazards of exposure to BPA has been recently published elsewhere [8]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to monitor the presence of BPA in daily liquid food leached from BPA-based food contact materials.

The traditional detection methods for BPA such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9,10], liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC–MS) [11,12], gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [13], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [14] require expensive equipment, considerable analysis time, experienced technicians, and complex sample pretreatment; therefore, their applications in on-site rapid analysis are extremely limited.

Fluorescence assay is a promising analytical technique with the advantages of less sample requirement, low cost, high sensitivity, less time consumption, and ease of handling [15,16]. Thus, in recent years, fluorescence assay has been widely used in biochemical, food, and environmental science. Several studies have been reported on the fluorescence determination of BPA [17–22]. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), a fascinating class of quantum dots (QDs) based on carbon materials, have attracted increasing attention in recent years because of their interesting properties such as water solubility, biocompatibility, good photostability, and low cytotoxicity. These properties make CDs good alternatives to QDs for sensing metal ions [23–25], small molecules [26,27], and other biospecies [28,29] in food, environmental, and biological samples.

Dual-emission fluorescent nanoparticles used for ratiometric fluorescence detection have attracted significant attention in recent years and are simply obtained by combining two different fluorophores in one nanoparticle, one fluorophore as the reference and another as the signal reporting unit [26,30–35]. Compared to organic fluorophores, dual-emission fluorescent nanoparticles do not need elaborate molecular design and complex synthesis, and designing and constructing a ratiometric fluorescence probe becomes simpler and more efficient.

In this study, dual-emission nanoparticles were prepared for the ratiometric detection of BPA. The CDs and Rhodamine B (RhB) were linked in silica nanoparticles, and the fluorescence intensity was quenched by potassium bromate (KBrO3) oxidation in HCl. However, it was found that the addition of BPA in the system inhibited the fluorescence quenching. Thus, a sensitive sensor was developed based on the inhibition effect on the KBrO3 oxidation of dual-emission silica nanoparticles. The selectivity and sensitivity of this ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor to BPA were carefully investigated. The feasibility of the use of dual-emission silica nanoparticles to determine BPA in the leached solution of different plastic products was also investigated.

Fluorescence was measured using a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out using a JEM-2011 TEM. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in KBr were recorded using a WQF-510 FT-IR spectrometer (Beijing Rayleigh Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China).

N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropylmethyldimethoxysilane (AEAPMS), aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), RhB, and anhydrous citric acid (A.R.) were purchased from Aladdin. Tetraethoxysilane (AR) and KBrO3 (AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). Bisphenol A (A.R.) was purchased from J&K Scientific (Beijing, China). Ethanol, NH4OH, and other reagents were purchased from Beijing chemical. Ultrapure water (18MΩcm−1)was used in all the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CD-Coated Dual-Emission Silica Nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The CD-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles were prepared by the reported method [35,36]. The entire preparation procedure includes three steps (Fig. 1 ):

Synthesis of CDs. Organosilane functionalized CDs were synthesized by the pyrolysis of anhydrous citric acid in AEAPMS at 230°C for 1min according to the Zhang's method [37]. After the purification by petroleum ether, 0.4mL of the obtained product was added to 1.6mL ethanol for the next experiment.

Preparation of RhB-doped silica nanoparticles. For this step, RhB was precoupled to APTES by the reaction between carboxylic group of rhodamine and amino group of APTES in ethanol under stirring for 24h at room temperature. The obtained RhB-APAES solution (100μL), TEOS (50μL), and ammonia (200μL) were added to ethanol (5mL), and then the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 8h. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was centrifuged, and the silica nanoparticles were collected and washed three times with ethanol. The obtained nanoparticles were ultrasonically dispersed in 5.0mL ethanol for 10min, and then 50μL TEOS and 100μL ammonia were added to the solution and stirred for 24h at room temperature. The resulting dye-doped silica nanoparticles were collected by centrifugation and washed three times with ethanol and redispersed into 5.0mL ethanol.

Preparation of CD-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles. The solution (3.0mL) of dye-doped silica nanoparticles obtained above was mixed with ammonia (100μL). After stirring for 30min, 10μL of CDs was added and stirred for 15h at room temperature. Then, the reaction mixture was centrifuged, and the obtained CD-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles were washed three times with ethanol and redispersed in ethanol at a concentration of 10mgmL−1 for the next use.

First, 1mL of a solution of CD-coated silica nanoparticles was mixed with 1mL HCl solution (2.0M) and 400μL of KBrO3 (6.0×10−5 M) in a 10mL graduated tube. Then, a certain amount of the BPA standard solution (or the sample solution) was added into the test tube. After diluting with ultrapure water to the required volume, the mixture was shaken thoroughly and then stand for 20min at room temperature prior to the fluorescence measurement. Simultaneously, a reagent blank was prepared (without adding the BPA standard solution or sample solution). The fluorescence intensities of the dual emission peaks (460nm and 572nm) for the test solution (F
460 and F
572 ) and reagent blank (F
0
460 and F
0
572 ) were directly measured using a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer with excitation at 360nm. Then, ΔF
460
=F
460
−
F
0
460 , ΔF
572
=F
572
−
F
0
572 , and the ΔF
460 /ΔF
572
values were calculated.

Polycarbonate plastic products were purchased from the local supermarket and snack-bar. The plastic products (cut into pieces and 15g of each product was used) were immersed in ultrapure water (100mL)and heated on an 80°C water bath for 1.5h. The corresponding soak solutions were collected and transferred into volumetric flasks for analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CD-Coated Dual-Emission Silica Nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The CD-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles were prepared by the reactions between the amine and carboxyl groups. The success of amidation reaction was confirmed by the FT-IR spectra [37], showing typical signals arising from a C ONR vibration at 1654cm−1 (C O) and 3456cm−1 (NH) in the reaction products. The peaks at 800 and 1100cm−1 can be attributed to the SiO and SiCH2 stretching vibrations. These results clearly indicate the successful synthesis of CD-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles. The TEM images showed that the average diameter of the CD-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles was ~260nm (Fig. 2 ). And the UV–visible absorption spectra of Carbon dots coated dual emission silica nanoparticles (1.0mgmL−1) was also collected (showed in Supplementary information, Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing Mechanism of the Ratiometric Nanosensor for BPA
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of the CD-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles-HCl-KBrO3-BPA systems (with different constituents) were scanned according to the experimental methods (Fig. 3 ). The CD-coated silica nanoparticles showed two emission peaks (460nm and 572nm) at a single excitation wavelength (360nm) and were assigned to the CD emission (460nm) and RhB emission (572nm), respectively. Fig. 3 shows that the fluorescence signals of both the emission peaks were quenched intensively by KBrO3 in acidic media. This can be attributed to the oxidation of the two fluorophores by KBrO3 in HCl. However, the fluorescence quenching effect was weakened when BPA was added to the system. This is probably because KBrO3 first reacted with BPA (because it is a stronger reductant than the two fluorophores); therefore, BPA inhibited the KBrO3 oxidation of the dual-emission fluorescent nanoparticles.

For the system containing 300ngmL−1 BPA, the effects of acid type and concentration, concentration of KBrO3, reaction temperature, and time on the ΔF of the system were investigated by a univariate approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of Acid Media and Concentration of the System
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of acid type on the ΔF of the system was investigated by using different acids (HCl, H2SO4, and H3PO4) in the system according to the experimental method. The results are shown in Fig. 4 . Clearly, ΔF is much larger in HCl medium than in H2SO4 and H3PO4 media. For the BPA sensing mechanism, the fluorescence quenching and fluorescence recovery were both attributed to the oxidation of nanofluorophore and BPA by KBrO3. And the experimental results could be explained from the fact that KBrO3 was a strong oxidant in acid media, and Cl− is the possible catalyst for the oxidation reaction [38]. Therefore, HCl was selected as the reaction medium. Further, the concentration of HCl was also optimized, and the results indicate that the maximum ΔF was obtained when 0.2M HCl was used as the reaction medium. Therefore, 0.2M HCl was selected as the reaction medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the Concentration of KBrO3
ce:section.ce:para: According to the sensing mechanism, the concentration of oxidant (KBrO3) is an important factor affecting the ΔF of the inhibiting system. Therefore, it is the determining factor for the sensitivity of the method based on the inhibiting system. The effect of KBrO3 concentration was investigated by varying it from 3.0×10−7 to 4.2×10−6 M. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 5 . The ΔF460 of the inhibiting system first increased as the concentration of KBrO3 increased from 3.0×10−7 to 2.4×10−6 M and then decreased with further increase in the concentration of KBrO3. Simultaneously, the ΔF572 of the inhibiting system changed slightly throughout a full testing concentration range of KBrO3. These results may be explained that groups on the surface of carbon dots(fluorescence emission peak 460nm) was easier reaction with KBrO3, so the ΔF572 was just slight increased with higher KBrO3 concentration. And when fluorescence of carbon dots was quenched completely by KBrO3, extra amount of KBrO3 would reaction with BPA leading to no effect of fluorescence recovery, which attributed to a great decrease of ΔF460. Therefore, 2.4×10−6 M KBrO3 was selected as the oxidant concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction Temperature and Time of the Inhibiting System
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of reaction temperature and time on ΔF were investigated (Fig. 6 ). Both the ΔF460 and ΔF572 of the system had similar trend when the reaction temperature was varied from room temperature to 90°C; they increased with the increase in temperature, however decreased significantly at higher temperatures (up to 90°C). This is probably because the inhibiting reaction was efficient at a higher temperature, whereas the bare fluorescence intensities of the CD-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles sharply decreased, thus decreasing the ΔF460 and ΔF572. Therefore, 20°C was selected as the reaction temperature.

The reaction time was also investigated at the optimal reaction temperature according to the experimental method. The results indicate that 20min was sufficient to obtain the maximum of both ΔF460 and ΔF572, and ΔF gradually decreased with a longer reaction time of >20min(showed in the Supplementary information, Fig. S2). As mentioned above, the Cl− is the possible catalyst for the oxidation reaction. BPA as an inhibitor for the oxidation system, it works for the catalyzing process, so longer reaction time could reduce BPA's inhibiting effect. Thus, 20min reaction time was used for the inhibiting system.

The effects of the potential interference from some metal ions, other oxidants, and reductants on the BPA sensing system were investigated. In these experiments, the solutions containing BPA (300ngmL−1) and the added interfering materials were treated according to the recommended procedure under the optimum conditions, and the tolerance limit of the coexisting materials, defined as the maximum concentration to achieve <5% variation of the fluorescence intensities for both the emission peaks, are shown in Table 1 . Many metals ions and phenols exhibited high tolerance limits; however, some strong oxidants such as KMnO4 and K2Cr2O7 exhibited much lower tolerance limits. This is because strong oxidants reacted with BPA and affected the inhibition of the oxidative quenching of nanosensors. Fortunately, a high concentration of strong oxidants is rare in most samples for BPA analysis.

Under the optimum experimental conditions, the concentration of BPA was linear with ΔF460/ΔF572 in the range 10–500ngmL−1. The linear regression equation of the working curve was ΔF460/ΔF572 =2.6942+0.01274C (ng mL−1), and the correlation coefficient (r) was 0.99469. According to the IUPAC definition, the detection limit (3σ) of this method was 0.80ngmL−1 for BPA, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 3.6% (c=60ng mL−1, n=7).

The proposed method was applied to determine BPA in the leached solution of different plastic products. The analytical results and recoveries of antibacterial products are shown in Table 2 . The recoveries for the leached solution samples were between 97.1% and 99.5%, and the RSDs were between 1.09 and 7.38 (%), indicating that the sensor has high accuracy, high sensitivity, and precision.

A sensitive ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor was developed to determine BPA based on the inhibition of the KBrO3 oxidation of CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles. The inhibition-based sensing system has many advantages such as rapid detection, good selectivity, high sensitivity, and a wide linear response range and has potential applications in the detection of BPA in the leached solutions of plastic products.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We sincerely acknowledge the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 21205028), Program for Science and Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (Grant no. 13HASTIT016) and the seed fund for Key Teacher of Henan University of Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2017.01.049.
